summary: The post examines why the tech industry perceives talent scarcity differently from other industries by proposing three distinct models of talent distribution: normal (process-driven companies like Toyota), Pareto (knowledge-work firms like McKinsey that seek top-percentile 'A-players'), and bimodal (creative and deep-tech companies that depend on irreplaceable 'linchpins'). Asparouhova argues that the '10x developer' concept differs fundamentally from McKinsey's 'top talent' because it describes singular individuals whose abilities can't be replicated, not just high performers on a bell curve. She explains that bimodal distribution is rare but especially relevant in software infrastructure and creative industries, where the possibility space is infinite and linchpins can 'see' solutions others cannot. The framework also accounts for how corporate cultures, management styles, and hiring practices differ across these models, and why software companies often struggle when transitioning from bimodal to Pareto distribution as they mature.
key_points: Three distinct talent distribution models (normal, Pareto, bimodal) explain different corporate cultures and hiring strategies better than a single 'top talent' framework
The '10x developer' concept reflects bimodal distribution—singular linchpins whose abilities are qualitatively unique—rather than Pareto distribution's quantitatively ranked A-players
Software's infinite possibility space and invisible dependencies create a much larger gap between average and exceptional engineers than in physical engineering disciplines
Most modern software companies are actually Pareto-distribution companies selling commoditized products, but the industry's culture still reflects its bimodal origins
Companies' talent distribution needs change over time—Google likely transitioned from bimodal to Pareto as it matured, possibly at the cost of its reputation for innovation
money_quotes: There are only [10-200] people in the world who can do what [highly-paid AI researcher] does. This is a very different statement from, say, 'We are trying to hire top AI researchers.'
Writing software has no such constraints. Solving problems with code has an infinite possibility space; what you can build, and how you build it, is only bound by imagination.

The gap between an average and exceptional software engineer is much bigger than that between an average and exceptional physical engineer, as an exceptional developer can 'see' possibilities that an average one cannot.

While historically, virtually all software companies were tackling difficult technical challenges, today, most probably look more like Pareto-distribution companies, and should follow a different management approach.

If a normal-distribution company is more like socialism – where employees see themselves as part of a bigger machine – the Pareto-distribution company is more like capitalism, where rewards are unevenly distributed, accruing to the best performers.

Silicon Valley skews more qualitative, favors lone 'creative genius' archetypes, and has a positive-sum approach to talent: linchpins could be lurking anywhere, and we surely haven't uncovered them all.
A team comprised of linchpins doesn't necessarily produce great outcomes, versus having teams that are each centered around a linchpin, who's paired with a supporting team.
key_insight: The tech industry's notion of talent scarcity reflects a bimodal distribution where irreplaceable 'linchpins' matter more than top-percentile 'A-players,' but most modern software companies have actually shifted to Pareto dynamics without updating their cultural assumptions.
